articles.title: 132-Million-Year-Old Dinosaur Tracks Rewrite Southern Africa's History
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered dinosaur tracks on a beach in South Africa that are 132 million years old. The tracks are the youngest ever found in southern Africa and show dinosaurs lived there long after massive volcanic eruptions. A team searching for a dinosaur tooth instead found dozens of footprints in a tiny rock area near Knysna. The site is only 40 meters long, but it suggests dinosaurs were common in the region during the Cretaceous period. The tracks are covered by the ocean at high tide twice a day.

articles.title: 100-Million-Year-Old Snake Fossil with Legs Rewrites Evolution Story
articles.summary: A fossil snake from Argentina, Najash rionegrina, lived 100 million years ago and had hind legs and a cheekbone that modern snakes lack. Scientists used micro-CT scans to study the skull in detail, correcting a 160-year-old misunderstanding about snake anatomy. The discovery challenges the old idea that snakes evolved from small burrowing lizards. Instead, early snakes were big-bodied and big-mouthed. The fossil shows snakes retained their hindlimbs for a long time before becoming limbless.

articles.title: First Lava Tube Found on Venus—Bigger Than Any on Earth
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered the first lava tube on Venus by reanalyzing radar data from NASA's Magellan probe from the 1990s. The tube, near the volcano Nyx Mons, is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide—larger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars. The discovery was made by spotting a collapsed skylight in the radar images. This finding will guide future missions like NASA's VERITAS and Europe's EnVision, which will study Venus with advanced radar. Venus's surface is extremely hot and has crushing pressure, so humans cannot explore the tube in person.
